claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in care for 7 weeks (within 8-week limit)
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child in care for 7 weeks (within 8-week limit); Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child in care for 7 weeks (within 8-week limit)
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for UK Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about your child's age:**

- Is your child **under 16 years old**?
- Is your child **between 16 and 19 years old**?
- Is your child **20 years old or over**?

Please let me know which age category applies to your child. If you have multiple children you'd like to claim for, we can go through them one at a time, or you can tell me all their ages and I'll help assess each one.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh hello, yes I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit. I just have the one child to ask about - my son Alex. He's 8 years old and lives at home with me. Can you let me know if I'd be eligible?

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Lovely to meet you! Thanks for sharing that information about Alex. Since he's 8 years old, he falls into the **under 16** category, which is great news for eligibility purposes.

Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check about Alex's work status:

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?**

Given that Alex is only 8 years old, I'd assume he's not in paid employment - but please confirm this for me so we can proceed with the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not working at all - he's only 8! He just goes to school like any other kid his age.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Of course! That makes perfect sense - I just need to confirm these things as part of the official assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Next question:

**Is Alex NOT receiving certain benefits in his own right?**

Specifically, this refers to benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit claimed in the child's own name. Again, given Alex is 8 years old and at school, I'd expect the answer is no - he's not receiving any such benefits - but please confirm.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, he doesn't receive any benefits in his own name. He's just a regular 8-year-old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, thank you for confirming!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question:

**Is Alex currently in an apprenticeship?**

Since Alex is 8 and attending regular school, I'm confident he's not in any apprenticeship programme. Can you just confirm that for me?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, of course not - he's only 8 and in primary school!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Absolutely, just dotting the i's and crossing the t's!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now we need to check your residency status. 

**Do you meet UK residency requirements?**

You qualify if **any one** of the following applies to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**
2. You have **settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have **pre-settled status** and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold)
4. You have **pre-settled status** and are actively seeking work
5. You have **pre-settled status** with sufficient financial resources
6. You have **pre-settled status** and are a student with sufficient resources
7. You have **pre-settled status** as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national
8. You're a **Crown servant** living abroad

Do any of these apply to you? For most people, simply living in the UK normally covers this requirement.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester. I've always lived here.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Wonderful, that's exactly what we need - living in the UK covers the residency requirement perfectly.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to confirm your responsibility for Alex.

**Are you responsible for Alex?**

You qualify if **any one** of the following applies:

1. **You live with Alex** (which you mentioned earlier - he lives at home with you)
2. You **contribute to his upkeep** (food, clothes, pocket money, housing, etc.)
3. You **pay maintenance** under a court order or agreement

Since you mentioned Alex lives at home with you in Manchester, I assume option 1 applies - can you confirm you live with him?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that confirms responsibility.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check about other potential claimants:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

For example, this could be another parent, a partner, or another family member. 

- If **no one else** is claiming for Alex, we can proceed
- If **someone else is claiming** but you've both agreed that you should be the one to claim instead, we can also proceed
- If there's a **dispute** about who should claim, HMRC would need to make the final decision

Which situation applies to you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. It's just me looking after him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, thank you for confirming that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Nearly there! I just need to check Alex's current living situation:

**Where is Alex currently living?**

- **At home with you** (normal living situation)
- **In hospital**
- **In local authority care**
- **In a foster care arrangement**

Since you mentioned he lives at home with you and goes to primary school, I assume it's a normal living situation - is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, actually, he has been in local authority care for a little while, but he still lives with me most of the time.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for letting me know about that. Since Alex has been in local authority care, I need to check a few more details about this arrangement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that detail. This is an important question:

**Has Alex been in local authority care for 8 weeks or less, OR does he spend at least 24 hours a week at home with you?**

You mentioned he still lives with you most of the time, which sounds like he may spend significant time at home. To qualify for Child Benefit when a child is in care:

- The child must have been in care for **8 weeks or less**, OR
- The child spends **at least 24 hours a week** at home with you

Additionally, you can continue to claim if Alex spends:
- At least **2 consecutive nights per week** at home, OR
- **7 or more days in a row** at home

Does any of this apply to Alex's situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's been in care for a couple of months now, but he does come home and stay with me - he's home quite a bit actually, more than just weekends.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. So Alex has been in care for more than 8 weeks (a couple of months), but it sounds like he spends quite a lot of time at home with you.

To confirm eligibility, I need to check: **Does Alex spend at least 24 hours a week at home with you?**

Since you mentioned he's home "quite a bit" and "more than just weekends," it sounds like this might be the case. Can you confirm that he's home for 24 hours or more each week?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, definitely - he's home with me more than 24 hours a week.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's great news - since Alex spends more than 24 hours a week at home with you, you can still qualify for Child Benefit even though he's been in care for more than 8 weeks.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
We're almost at the end of the assessment. One final question:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, are you covering Alex's costs yourself without the council paying for his accommodation or upkeep? 

Generally, if the local council is paying towards a child's accommodation or maintenance, you cannot receive Child Benefit for that child. Can you clarify whether the council is contributing financially to Alex's care?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything towards his accommodation or maintenance - I'm covering everything myself.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent news! That's all the questions I need to ask.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## 🎉 Great news! You ARE eligible for Child Benefit for Alex!

Based on our assessment, you meet all the eligibility requirements:

✅ Alex is under 16 years old  
✅ He's not working  
✅ He's not receiving benefits in his own name  
✅ He's not in an apprenticeship  
✅ You live in the UK  
✅ You're responsible for Alex and he lives with you  
✅ No one else is claiming Child Benefit for him  
✅ Although Alex is in local authority care, he spends more than 24 hours a week at home with you  
✅ The council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance  

---

### Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** (or adoption certificate)
4. **Payments** are made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

📌 **Important:** If you or a partner earn over **£60,000**, be aware of the High Income Child Benefit Charge. You can still claim, but you may need to pay some or all of it back through tax.

You can apply here: [https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim)

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old (under 16), which qualifies automatically for the age requirement. He is not working, not receiving benefits in his own name, and not in an apprenticeship. The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester) and meets residency requirements. The claimant lives with Alex and is responsible for him. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Although Alex has been in local authority care for more than 8 weeks, he spends more than 24 hours per week at home with the claimant, which satisfies the exception rule. The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their son Alex. Despite Alex being in local authority care for a couple of months, the claimant qualifies because Alex spends more than 24 hours per week at home and the council is not contributing to his accommodation or maintenance costs.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Has the child been in care for 8 weeks or less, OR do they spend at least 24 hours a week at home?
response.help_text: Child Benefit continues for up to 8 weeks in care. Exception if child spends 24+ hours/week at home. Can claim again if child spends 2 consecutive nights/week or 7+ days in a row at home.

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 8 years old (under 16), which qualifies automatically for the age requirement. He is not working, not receiving benefits in his own name, and not in an apprenticeship. The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester) and meets residency requirements. The claimant lives with Alex and is responsible for him. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Although Alex has been in local authority care for more than 8 weeks, he spends more than 24 hours per week at home with the claimant, which satisfies the exception rule. The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their son Alex. Despite Alex being in local authority care for a couple of months, the claimant qualifies because Alex spends more than 24 hours per week at home and the council is not contributing to his accommodation or maintenance costs.
